# Questions in Livestream 154

Live Stream #154: A Brief Question of Logic

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:55</a> | Is there anything biblical archaeology is especially unlikely ever to discover? | He says the ark of the covenant is the clearest example and argues it was likely destroyed rather than waiting to be rediscovered. | He names the ark of the covenant as the item he most expects biblical archaeology never to find. He doubts it still exists, thinks Nebuchadnezzar II probably destroyed it, and appeals to Jeremiah as indicating it will not come to mind or be recovered again. He therefore treats ark-hunting claims as futile and explicitly rejects Ron Wyatt's claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=767" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:47</a> | Did ancient kings ever exile brothers instead of killing them as succession threats? | He says Egypt usually redirected brothers into administration or the military, unlike harsher Mesopotamian patterns. | He distinguishes Egypt from Mesopotamian, Ottoman, and similar royal patterns. In Egypt, royal brothers were often sent into scribal, administrative, military, or chariotry roles while the father still reigned, which distributed them across the country and made them less threatening. He adds that Egyptian succession normally prioritized the king's sons, especially sons of the great queen, so brothers usually were not viable heirs except in rare cases when no royal children could succeed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=1079" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:59</a> | What kind of cross was Jesus crucified on? | He rules out a simple stake or X-shape and says it was probably between a Latin cross and a tau form. | He explains the options as a Latin cross, tau-shaped cross, simple stake, or X-shaped form. He rules out the simple stake because the terminology and the carried crossbeam imply more than a single vertical beam, and he treats the X-shape as outside the likely options. Since the charge against Jesus was nailed above him, he says it was probably not a pure tau cross, but he remains unsure whether it was a Latin cross, a tau cross, or something between the two. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=1277" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:17</a> | What criticisms do you have of postmodernism, especially social constructs and skeptical subjectivity? | He says postmodernity has some useful insights, but he rejects Marxist social-construct claims and radical skeptical subjectivity. | He says postmodernity is not wholly worthless because its indeterminate worldview fits some modern physics better than strict modernist mechanism, and its attention to metanarrative can be a real advance. His main criticisms are the Marxist social-construct approach, which he describes as category-blurring, and skeptical subjectivity, especially the claim that there is no truth. He expects later philosophy to keep what is useful and discard those parts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=1544" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:44</a> | What are your thoughts on epistemic particularism? | He contrasts it with epistemic methodism and says particularism works well for innate or moral knowledge that does not first require formal justification. | He defines epistemic particularism as the view that someone can know a belief is true before giving a formal justification for it, in contrast to methodism, where a criterion must justify the belief first. He argues methodism leans on scientific naturalism, which is not adequate as the only source of knowledge. Particularism, in his view, makes sense for moral and non-empirical knowledge, such as knowing murder is wrong without first constructing an argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=1769" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:29</a> | How can people know what is true if they cannot reach 100 percent certainty? | He argues people work with probability and confidence rather than absolute certainty, with faith bridging the remaining uncertainty. | He says the inability to reach absolute certainty does not mean there is no truth. People instead work with confidence based on probability and then trust the remaining gap, which he links to faith. His flashlight example illustrates ordinary life: someone can be confident it is a flashlight and will turn on based on past experience, even though batteries or parts could fail. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=2078" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:38</a> | What are his thoughts on Heaven's Gate? | He treats it as a UFO-centered suicide cult and briefly sketches its origins and later survival. | He identifies Heaven's Gate as a UFO cult founded by Applewhite and Nettles in the 1970s, mixing Christian apocalypticism, monastic denial of the flesh, and New Age ideas such as ascended mastery. He describes its mass suicide around the Hale-Bopp comet as tied to the belief that members needed to leave before the gate closed. He also notes that, like some other new religious movements, the group or its successor identity continued after the deaths. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=2286" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:06</a> | Does he have a black belt, and does he have any stories about it? | He says the answer is complicated, confirms there are stories, and declines to share them. | His answer is intentionally brief. He says the black-belt question is complicated, acknowledges that there are interesting stories, and then explicitly declines to tell them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=2333" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:53</a> | What is love? | He says in Christian terms love is more a chosen act of doing good than merely a feeling. | He contrasts modern ideas of love as feeling with earlier and Christian usage, where love is primarily a choice to do good for another person. Loving a neighbor or enemy does not require warm feelings; it means choosing to exercise goodness toward them. He says the feeling-centered idea developed later in late antiquity and the early medieval period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=2505" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:45</a> | What are his thoughts on theosophy? | He describes it as a nineteenth-century spiritualist movement that helped seed occult and New Age movements. | He traces theosophy to Blavatsky and calls it a Western form of imported Eastern religion. He says it includes karma, reincarnation, and ascended masters, where spiritually advanced souls become powerful teachers who can be contacted for guidance on the right spiritual path. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=2702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:02</a> | Which cults does he find most interesting? | He says the stranger groups are often most interesting to study and singles out vampire cults as especially fascinating. | He says the weirder a cult is, the more interesting he tends to find it. He previously found Mormonism fascinating because of its strangeness, but currently singles out vampire cults such as the House of Siaza and UVP. What interests him is that groups with different roots, including Wiccan, Satanist, or gnostic backgrounds, can converge on similar practices despite coming from different religious roots. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=2901" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:21</a> | Is psychic vampirism like life force? | He says it is similar, but in reverse: taking energy rather than giving it. | He compares the idea to a healer putting energy into someone, then describes psychic vampirism as the opposite movement. In his summary, the claim is that the psychic vampire sucks the life or energy out of another person rather than supplying it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=2941" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:01</a> | Do vampire cults drink each other's blood? | He says some do, usually in very small quantities mixed with wine. | He says some groups practice going sanguinous, but describes the amount as tiny, about a thimbleful. He adds that the blood is usually mixed with red wine because blood is hard on the stomach, making it easier to consume. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=3331" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:31</a> | What books build strong arguments against major world religions? | He recommends first learning each religion from the inside through solid introductory and specific studies, then building arguments from that understanding. | He does not recommend a single book that refutes all major religions. Instead, he advises starting with a good introductory world-religions text to understand what each religion believes, then using specific treatments for Buddhism, Sikhism, Hinduism, Islam, or whichever tradition is in view. He stresses that the best arguments come after learning the belief system from the inside and that each religion needs case-by-case treatment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=3603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:03</a> | What is the meaning of Ephesians 3:8-10? | He says the hidden mystery of God's gospel is now unveiled universally to Jew and Gentile through the church. | He reads the passage in light of Ephesians 3:6, where Gentiles are fellow heirs through the gospel. He explains the mystery as something hidden in God and wrapped in earlier cultural forms but now revealed openly. In his summary, the universal power and riches of Christ are made known through the church to all peoples and even to heavenly rulers and authorities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=3851" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:11</a> | Are there YouTube channels or apologists who address other religions deeply? | He names a few Islamic apologetics examples but says many other religions remain underserved. | He mentions David Wood and Sentinel Apologetics in connection with Islam, then says the YouTube apologetics space has many duplicate channels and many gaps. He would like to see deeper apologetics work for Buddhists, Sikhs, neopagans, Wiccans, Hindus, and similar groups, but says that work should come from people called to care for those communities rather than from people seeking attention. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=4215" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:15</a> | Where can someone find scholarly articles and keep up with excavations in Egypt and the Levant? | He says the latest work is in academic journals, especially relevant society journals, though they are expensive and sometimes mirrored later on Academia.edu. | He says the newest research appears in academic journals, naming the Bulletin of ASOR for the Levant and the Journal of the American Research Center in Egypt for Egypt. He warns that society memberships and journal access are expensive, which is why he usually does not recommend that route casually. Books are useful but tend to lag behind current research, while Academia.edu can host published articles but requires sifting because it is not curated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=4480" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:40</a> | Was Paul's apostleship only like the seventy in Luke 10, and how can Paul's canonicity be defended? | He says Luke 10 names "others," not apostles, and points to apostolic recognition such as 2 Peter's treatment of Paul's writings. | He argues Luke 10 does not make the seventy apostles, since the text calls them "others" and places them among wider followers of Jesus rather than the apostolic group. For Paul's recognition, he appeals to 2 Peter 3, where Peter refers to Paul's letters, the wisdom given to Paul, and people distorting Paul's writings as they do the rest of Scripture. He also points to early church evidence, especially 1 Clement, which he says was written close to Paul and quotes several Pauline letters, including 1 Corinthians. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=5292" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:12</a> | How were ancient hours affected by seasonal daylight, and why was lamb's blood used for Passover? | He says daylight and nighttime were each divided into twelve variable-length hours, and lamb blood was used because lambs were widely accessible. | He explains that ancient hours were not fixed-length modern hours. Daylight was divided into twelve hours and nighttime into twelve hours, so winter daylight hours were shorter and summer daylight hours were longer, with the inverse happening at night. For Passover, he says lambs were practical because Semitic families in Egypt commonly had sheep, while cattle were expensive; poorer families could use goats, but lambs were broadly accessible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=5574" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:54</a> | Which pharaohs did Moses live through, and does the Bible imply only two pharaohs in Moses' time? | He says the text does not require only two; it uses literary amalgamation for oppression pharaohs and one exodus pharaoh. | He says Moses' 120 years may be a symbolic arrangement, probably closer to about ninety years. The Bible does not require only two pharaohs; rather, readers often infer an oppression pharaoh and an exodus pharaoh. He describes the oppression pharaoh as a literary amalgamation spanning multiple rulers and names five kings in Moses' lifetime: Ay, Horemheb, Ramesses I, Seti I, and Ramesses II. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=5876" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:56</a> | Who are the Danu or Denyen in Genesis 14:14? | He says this is not the tribe of Dan but a northern Syrian or Anatolian-related location or group. | He reads Genesis 14 as using topographic identifiers and argues that Dan in this passage is north of Damascus, not the later Israelite tribal territory. He identifies it with Danu or Denyen, a group or area connected with Anatolian or Hittite-related peoples and mentioned in the Amarna letters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=6132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:12</a> | What does "but now they are holy" mean in 1 Corinthians 7:14? | He says it means the believer sanctifies the marriage so the children are legitimate, not that unequal marriage is ideal. | He explains the passage as addressing marriages where one spouse is a believer and the other is not. In his reading, Paul is not endorsing pursuit of an unbelieving spouse, but saying the existing marriage is valid and should not be dissolved simply because one spouse is an unbeliever; the children are therefore holy or legitimate within that consecrated marriage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=6536" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:56</a> | What if the unbelieving spouse takes three more wives? | He says that would not be a legitimate marriage where bigamy is illegal, and the abandoned spouse is not compelled to accept it. | He distinguishes contexts where polygamy is legally permitted from places like North America where bigamy is illegal. In an illegal setting, he says additional marriages are not legitimate, and if the spouse goes off to live with those wives, the original spouse is not under compulsion to go along with it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=6798" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:18</a> | Is there a logical problem with deconstruction? | He says deconstruction is not really a logical system so much as a broader way of approaching life and meaning. | He says deconstruction is not a logical system but a form of practical philosophy about how a person lives. In the context discussed, it means tearing down an existing belief structure to its foundation and then, in theory, reconstructing something better. His concern is practical rather than strictly logical: people often deconstruct but never reconstruct. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=6895" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:55</a> | How do we know Job is Iron Age literature? | He says Job's references to Arameans fit the period after Mitanni's collapse and before Aram fully reformed. | He grounds the dating in Job's references to Arameans as raiding tribal groups. In the Bronze Age, he says Aram was part of the cohesive Mitanni empire, but after Mitanni collapsed it became a loose collection of city-states before reforming as the kingdom of Aram. Job's setting fits that intermediate period, so he says it cannot be earlier than early Iron Age I, though it could be later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=7029" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:09</a> | What are his thoughts on The Action Bible? | He dislikes reducing the Bible to entertainment value, even though he is not against comic-book formats as such. | He distinguishes comic-book presentation from entertainment-driven Bible products. He is not against Bible stories in comic-book form, but he criticizes The Action Bible for emphasizing fun, multimedia, and entertainment value. His concern is that if the Bible is reduced to entertainment, readers may abandon it when something more entertaining appears and never develop the seriousness needed for sustained reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=7385" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:05</a> | What is a discernment ministry? | He says it is a ministry focused on distinguishing truth from error, but warns that such ministries can fall into caricature. | He defines a discernment ministry as one focused on using biblical discernment to tell truth from lies and biblical teaching from unbiblical teaching. Examples include counter-cult work or critiques of movements such as Word of Faith. He sees a legitimate role for such ministries, but warns that they can fall into straw-manning or caricaturing the groups they oppose; he also broadens discernment to practical church decisions such as benevolence and elder appointment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=7833" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:33</a> | Why is Egypt so prone to dictators, and was it still a dictatorship under Christian rule? | He says Egypt has long been a military state, and Christian Egypt was still under Roman or Byzantine imperial rule. | He says Egyptian power has usually belonged to whoever controlled the strongest military, with only a brief democratic opening during the Arab Spring. When asked about Christian rule, he says Egypt was then a Roman province and later under the Eastern Roman or Byzantine emperor, so it still functioned under a dictator. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=8034" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:54</a> | What are his thoughts on the Quran saying Pharaoh ordered Moses crucified? | He says, if the claim is being represented correctly, it is anachronistic because Egypt did not use crucifixion in Moses' period. | He cautions that he has not checked the Quran passage himself, but answers the historical claim directly. He says New Kingdom Egypt used methods such as throat-slitting or strangulation for capital punishment, not crucifixion; wood was too valuable, and crucifixion only enters Egypt under the Romans. On that basis he calls the claim historically inaccurate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=8247" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:27</a> | How can the Nile be deadly but make the land fertile? | He says the water can carry dangerous parasites, while the flood silt fertilized the soil. | He distinguishes the hazards of drinking or swimming in Nile water from the fertility produced by inundation. The annual flood brought black silt full of organic material and minerals onto the fields, but the water itself could carry parasites and contaminate produce if handling and washing were poor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=8648" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:08</a> | How often did kings kill their brothers? | He says it depended on the culture, with Assyrian, Babylonian, and Ottoman examples much harsher than Egyptian patterns. | He gives a short culture-dependent answer. In places such as Babylon, Assyria, Syria, and especially the Ottoman Empire, he says it could happen regularly at a new monarch's accession, even describing each coronation as bringing a line of coffins. This contrasts with his earlier comments that Egypt usually handled royal brothers differently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=8688" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:48</a> | What does he mean by saying eunuchs were crushed rather than castrated? | He says some ancient Near Eastern eunuchs were made by a crushing procedure because full castration carried a high infection risk. | He explains that in the ancient Near East full castration carried a severe infection risk, so some eunuchs were made by crushing the testicles instead. He describes it as brutal and traumatic, but says it was more survivable than cutting because infection killed so many people in the ancient world. He estimates, cautiously, that perhaps around eighty percent survived the crushing procedure, while China practiced castration because traditional medicine had treatments that could reduce infection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=8963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:23</a> | Was it kosher for Sabaean incense to be burned in the temple, and is there news on the Amorite tablets? | He says imported incense was ritually acceptable, and he has seen no major follow-up because the Amorite-tablet article was strong. | He says incense was treated as ritually pure regardless of origin, and temple demand exceeded local supply, so imports were not a problem. On the Amorite tablets, he says he has not seen significant new developments and interprets that as likely because the article was well done enough to become the defining treatment, apart from possible future fine points. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=9147" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:27</a> | How should the changing iconography of seraphim and cherubim in the Bible be understood? | He says the imagery shifts with cultural context, from Egyptian-like forms toward Mesopotamian-influenced forms. | He contrasts Bronze Age imagery, such as winged female figures, with later six-winged and multi-headed forms associated with Babylonian and Mesopotamian settings. His explanation is that visions are described through analogical language and cultural expectations: the prophet's mind fills in spiritual realities using the imagery available in that cultural world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=9426" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:06</a> | What is a consecutive vav, and how does Stephen rely on it in Acts? | He says a consecutive vav marks temporal progression, and Stephen reads Genesis 11-12 as Abraham leaving after Terah's death. | He connects Acts 7:4 to Genesis 11:32 and 12:1. A conjunctive vav can work like a normal "and" or "but," but a consecutive vav indicates progression in time, which he paraphrases as "after that." He says Stephen correctly reads the consecutive vav in Genesis 12:1 to mean that God called Abraham out after Terah died, and he notes that Hebrew pointing helps distinguish the form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=9773" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:53</a> | What are his thoughts on Karl Popper's demarcation problem? | He says the problem of distinguishing science from pseudoscience remains real, but he cannot address recent Popper scholarship in depth. | He defines the demarcation problem as deciding how to tell science from pseudoscience, noting that a person usually needs enough knowledge of the field to recognize a quack. He uses Ron Wyatt as his example of pseudoscience being accepted because audiences cannot evaluate the method. He says recent work on the problem exists but he has not read enough of it to comment deeply. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=10458" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:18</a> | How should Ezekiel's described angels be viewed? | He says the description is functional and analogical rather than a literal physical portrait. | He says the many wings, eyes, and directional features communicate function by analogy: support, modesty, shielding the throne from impurity, constant sight, power, and movement. The point is to help readers understand what the beings are like, not to give a literal anatomical description. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=10534" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:34</a> | What advice does he give when someone doubts Christianity and would prefer agnosticism? | He says doubt is normal, but one should also examine the consequences and evidence for agnosticism. | He admits that he has periods of doubt too and says doubt can be healthy if it also questions itself. He argues that agnosticism may feel easier in the short term, but in his view it has long-term epistemic and moral problems, often living off the afterglow of Christianity. His practical advice is patience and careful comparison of both sides, including whether the evidence gives enough confidence even without certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=11137" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:37</a> | Who is Agur son of Jakeh in Proverbs 30? | He says the text gives too little information; Agur may be a collector or compiler of proverbs, but his identity is unknown. | He says Proverbs 30 gives only one reference to Agur son of Jakeh, so his identity cannot really be established. Agur may mean someone who collects, and Proverbs 30 appears to be a collection of sayings, so he may have compiled proverbs from multiple sources. Beyond that possible role, he says nothing definite is known about either Agur or Jakeh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=11206" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:46</a> | How would he respond to someone saying Jesus went to hell to get the keys from Satan? | He rejects the claim, distinguishing Hades from hell and saying Satan had no keys or rightful title to humanity. | He rejects the prophecy-style claim and says the Bible does not teach that Jesus went to hell in that sense. He distinguishes Hades from hell, describing Hades as compartmentalized, with a good place and a bad place, and says Jesus went to announce secured salvation to those in the good place rather than to suffer. He also denies that Satan held keys or title to humanity, describing Satan instead as a squatter who occupied what did not belong to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=11781" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:21</a> | Under historic premillennialism, is there a necessary distinction between the church and Israel? | He says the distinction has often appeared historically, but he does not see it as necessary to the view. | He says historic premillennialism has often made a distinction between the church and Israel, especially in the era of the early church fathers. However, he sees that as historically conditioned by tension and violence between Christians and Judaism rather than as a necessary element of the eschatological view. In his judgment, someone can hold historic premillennialism without maintaining a required church-Israel distinction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=12043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:43</a> | How should someone seeking assurance respond to thoughts of ending life to see whether Christ is real? | He says ending one's life is never the answer and points instead to living by faith and serving Christ. | He handles the question cautiously and says ending one's life is never a proper path to assurance. Assurance is sought by living an effective life for Christ, walking by faith, and seeing God work through one's life over time. He adds that if someone is seriously considering self-harm or has a plan, they need immediate help from mental-health professionals or a suicide-prevention hotline. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=12504" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:24</a> | Does self-titling as a prophet or apostle disqualify someone as a source of doctrine? | He says not automatically, but it should raise concern; doctrine and context still have to be judged. | He says the title itself does not automatically disqualify someone because some Christian traditions use prophet or apostle as ministerial or liturgical titles, similar to deacon or pastor. Self-applying such a title to elevate oneself is a red flag, but the decisive test is the person's doctrine. A person with no title may teach sound doctrine, and a person with a lofty title may not. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=12709" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:49</a> | Will physical evidence ever prove the exodus beyond reasonable doubt? | He says the evidence has already crossed reasonable doubt in his view, though unreasonable doubts can always be piled on. | He says plagues and conquest-tablet evidence would be difficult to nail down, but argues that existing evidence already points to people disappearing from one Egyptian setting and appearing in Canaan by Merneptah's reign. He distinguishes ordinary uncertainty from unreasonable doubt and says the cumulative evidence has crossed the threshold for a reasonable person, even if absolute proof is too high a standard. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=13133" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:53</a> | What is the second most reasonable explanation for Israelite origins if the exodus is rejected? | He says a later Amorite infiltration may be the next option, but only after ignoring a great deal of Egyptian-pattern evidence. | He argues that Egyptian-pattern evidence makes alternative explanations difficult, citing four-room houses at Avaris, avoidance of pork, purity practices, and furniture patterns. If all of that evidence is ignored, he says the next-best option might be a later second wave of Amorite infiltration, but he treats that as weak. He explicitly rejects an internal Canaanite origin as a good explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=13293" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:41:33</a> | Were Egyptian-influenced practices in Israelite law retained to set Israel apart from Canaanite culture? | He says Sinai involved keeping what was good from Egypt and discarding what was bad. | He frames the law as a winnowing of practices after the exodus: some Egyptian patterns, such as avoiding pork and ritual washing before approaching sacred space, were retained, while immoral or idolatrous practices were rejected. In his summary, Israel was being told what to keep and what to discard as it was set apart. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=13446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:06</a> | Are elders and pastors biblically distinct? | He says there is no biblical distinction between elders, bishops, and what later terminology calls pastors. | He says elders should have the qualifications and teaching responsibilities associated with pastors: teaching, rebuking, correcting, and training in righteousness. In his view, the Bible does not separate elder, bishop, and pastor as different offices in the way later church vocabulary sometimes does. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=13562" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:46:02</a> | How can readers tell whether Old Testament numbers are literal? | He says context helps, but numbers such as 7, 12, and 40 can be idiomatic or symbolic. | He points to forty years as a known ancient Near Eastern idiom and warns against automatically taking patterned numbers literally. For census texts such as Numbers 1, he says the issue is controversial, with literal, clan, soldier, and genre interpretations, so context helps but does not always settle the question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=13809" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:09</a> | What are his thoughts on Hyperborea? | He says he knows little about it beyond it being a mythological northern people in Greek tradition. | He looks it up during the stream and identifies Hyperborea as a people from Greek legend or mythology associated with the north. He says it is outside his wheelhouse and that he has no substantive comment beyond that limited identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=13980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:00</a> | Are the Hyksos the actual Israelites? | He says no; the Hyksos were Amorites but not Israelites, though some Hyksos-related people may have joined Israel. | He distinguishes Amorites broadly from Israelites: Israelites were Amorites in a sense, but not all Amorites were Israelites. He says the Hyksos belonged to an earlier Amorite wave in the Delta and ceased to be a viable political force long before the exodus. Some may have intermarried and joined the mixed multitude, but he rejects the exodus as merely the splitting of a Hyksos empire. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=14338" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:58:58</a> | How much of the Bible is against gnosticism? | He says relatively little directly addresses gnosticism; the New Testament touches it only tangentially, while the church fathers engage it more. | He says relatively little of the Bible directly argues against gnosticism, though one can infer many biblical ideas that oppose it. He describes gnosticism as developing from Egyptian religion combined with Platonic thought and mystery religion, but says it was not a major Christian problem until roughly the mid-second century. Therefore the New Testament only touches it tangentially, while the church fathers address it more directly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=14566" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:46</a> | Would people from different cultures report different things from the same ghost sighting? | He says yes, cultural context can shape details even when people report the same event. | He says communal ghost sightings can involve several people seeing the same entity but describing it differently, sometimes because of cultural context or personal focus. As an example, he says most ghost reports omit feet or shoes, but someone especially interested in shoes might notice and describe them in detail. He also notes that groups often share similar backgrounds, so their reports may be broadly similar while differing in details. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=14724" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:24</a> | How is "beyond reasonable doubt" defined? | He defines it as a state where no normal, rational, even-minded person would have a good reason to disbelieve. | He first defines a doubt as a reason to disbelieve, then a reasonable doubt as one a rational and even-minded person would hold. Beyond reasonable doubt therefore does not eliminate all wild or unreasonable doubts; it means the remaining doubts are not the sort a normal, fair-minded person should treat as decisive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=14920" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:08:40</a> | Did kings also kill their sisters? | He says generally no, because sisters were less direct succession threats and could serve dynastic marriage purposes. | He says sisters were generally not killed when a brother came to the throne. They could be used in royal marriages and treaty arrangements, especially if daughters were unavailable, and they usually posed little succession threat because they lacked the same military and dynastic base as brothers. He allows that unusually connected women could be exceptions, but says sisters were normally far less dangerous to a king. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/kH3qHY_b3BU?t=15079" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:11:19</a> | Did women also get beatings in Egypt? | He says yes; Egyptian authorities could beat women as well as men, including high-status women in legal contexts. | He says women were not exempt from beatings in Egypt. He points to tomb-robbery papyri where a high-status woman, titled the citizen Isis, could be beaten in the same way as men. His summary is that Egyptian authorities were equal-opportunity abusers, so a woman confronted by officials could also expect physical punishment. |
